title: Bitcoin: We Really Do Live in a Simulation
author: IntoTheCryptoverse
summary_bullets: Hey everyone and thanks for jumping back into the cryptoverse.
Today we're going to talk about Bitcoin and how it still really really feels like we live in a simulation.
Give the video a thumbs up and check out Into the Cryptoverse Premium at into the cryptoverse.com.
What's fascinating about Bitcoin is how repetitive cycle behavior is.
And every single time it's like, is this really going to happen again?
And then sure enough, it happens again.
And if if you watch the video a couple days ago, you know what where we were discussing this early March rally.
We've been talking about it for a couple of weeks.

And one of the reasons we've been talking about it is if you just look at the year-to- date ROI of Bitcoin and look at 2026 and you compare that to the average of prior midterm years and you add on one standard deviation from that average, we notice that Bitcoin was trading below where it normally does and that there's normally a little bit of an uptick in March after setting a low in February.
And sure enough, without fail, it happened again.

extracted_rules: And if if you watch the video a couple days ago, you know what where we were discussing this early March rally.

And one of the reasons we've been talking about it is if you just look at the year-to- date ROI of Bitcoin and look at 2026 and you compare that to the average of prior midterm years and you add on one standard deviation from that average, we notice that Bitcoin was trading below where it normally does and that there's normally a little bit of an uptick in March after setting a low in February.
So, if you go into a bare market early on last year, it was tough, you know, sort of bringing you guys the bare market message when many didn't want to hear it.
One of the reasons it was so easy to be a bear was because Q4 of the post having year when Bitcoin tops, right?
All right, that's always when Bitcoin tops Q4 of every post having year.
For instance, if you panic sold Bitcoin on the first wick down in November, you probably felt like a fool if you sold it at 80K.
you really felt like you made a mistake when it was trading back at $98,000, but then ultimately the floor dropped out and we went to 60K.
But one counterpoint to that in compar if you think that we can't compare it to 2022 because of that.
If you think about it, the 2021 late 21 high was not a euphoric top.
Late January, late February, that's normally when markets find a low for a little while in midterm years.
In 2014, when Bitcoin dropped, you know, to $400 by February and then it rallied back up to 670.
When Bitcoin went to 5,800 in February of 2018, was that the low?
In 2022, when it found this low in February, was that the low?
If people feel like they missed out, they're going to get upset.
What if it's like this move here where you get a wick down and then you start to go up and then you just sell off again?
In fact, if you zoom in, what you'll notice is that Bitcoin dropped.
But you don't know when that's going to occur.

I mean you could have thought we were going to go to the bare market resistance band back in May of 2022 when it was at you know 35,000 but then Bitcoin it started to go and then it just capitulated again and then by the time it hit it Bitcoin was at 20K you know.
Yeah, we don't know if it's going to be in the 80s.
What if it hits it in the 70s later on or if in the 60s and if in like four four months or something?

strategy_components.description: Bitcoin rallied up, got rejected, put in a lower high and came down.
a a counter trend rally to a lower high, a low in February, another low in February, rally into the first week of March, capitulate into April.
Like I would say the sort of the best case scenario for a counter trend rally is to go to the bare market resistance band.
I I I think it's most likely going to get rejected um in March.
